name: Shiva's Strength & Athletic
description: An 8-week, 4-day athletic strength program built around pull-ups, push-ups, dips, squats, deadlifts, clean and press, planks, and carries — with Surya Namaskar warm-ups and optional incline treadmill/stair finishers. Suitable for everyone; beginner substitutions (assisted pull-ups/dips) are noted on each day.
notes: General 8-week structure: Week 1 technique and baseline, Week 2 add reps, Week 3 add a small amount of weight or reduce assistance, Week 4 deload, Week 5 increase volume, Week 6 increase weight or difficulty, Week 7 hardest training week, Week 8 progress assessment. Progress at your own pace using your own entered weights — this program provides targets and guidance, not automatic load changes.

weeks.title: Week 1
weeks.notes: Learn each movement with strict form before adding load or reps.

weeks.title: Week 2
weeks.notes: Same weights as Week 1; add 1-2 reps per set where you can.

weeks.title: Week 3
weeks.notes: Small load increase on compounds, or one step down in pull-up/dip assistance.

weeks.title: Week 4
weeks.notes: Reduce sets and intensity to recover before the second half of the program.

weeks.title: Week 5
weeks.notes: Add a set to your main compound lifts.

weeks.title: Week 6
weeks.notes: Push loads again; advanced trainees can add weighted pull-ups/dips.

weeks.title: Week 7
weeks.notes: Highest planned effort of the program — keep form strict.

weeks.title: Week 8
weeks.notes: Retest a few key lifts/reps and compare against Week 1.

name: StrongLifts 5x5
description: A classic beginner barbell program: two alternating full-body workouts (Squat/Bench/Row and Squat/Press/Deadlift), 3 days a week, adding weight every session.
notes: Alternate Workout A and Workout B every session (not every week) — a typical block looks like A, B, A, then B, A, B. Add 5 lb (2.5 kg) to each lift every time you complete all reps; if you fail a lift three sessions in a row, cut the weight on that lift by 10% and build back up.

weeks.notes: Add a small amount of weight to each lift every session you complete all reps; if a lift fails three sessions in a row, deload it 10% and build back up.

name: Starting Strength
description: Mark Rippetoe's classic novice barbell program: two alternating workouts built around the squat, press, bench, deadlift, and chin-ups, 3 days a week.
notes: Alternate Workout A and Workout B every session — a typical block looks like A, B, A, then B, A, B. Add weight to the squat almost every session; add weight to the press and bench a little more slowly once they get harder.

name: Full Body Beginner
description: A simple full-body session repeated 3 times a week — a gentler introduction to structured training than a barbell-only program.
notes: Leave 1-2 reps in reserve on every set while you're learning the movements. Once every set feels easy at the top of its rep range, add weight next session.

weeks.title: Week 1
weeks.notes: Add a small amount of weight to each lift every session you complete all reps; if a lift fails three sessions in a row, deload it 10% and build back up.

name: Upper/Lower Split
description: A 4-day split alternating upper-body and lower-body sessions — a common next step after a full-body beginner routine.
notes: Train Upper/Lower on two days, rest a day, then repeat — a common pattern is Mon/Tue train, Wed off, Thu/Fri train, weekend off. Progress each lift independently: add weight when you hit the top of the rep range on every set.

weeks.title: Week 1
weeks.notes: Add a small amount of weight to each lift every session you complete all reps; if a lift fails three sessions in a row, deload it 10% and build back up.

name: Push Pull Legs (6-Day)
description: The classic 6-day bodybuilding split — push, pull, and legs, run twice through the week — for lifters who can recover from higher training frequency.
notes: Push = chest/shoulders/triceps, Pull = back/biceps, Legs = quads/hamstrings/glutes/calves. Take a rest day wherever it fits your week — most lifters run 6 on, 1 off.

weeks.title: Week 1
weeks.notes: Add a small amount of weight to each lift every session you complete all reps; if a lift fails three sessions in a row, deload it 10% and build back up.

name: Wendler 5/3/1 (4-Day)
description: Jim Wendler's percentage-based strength cycle: one main lift a day (press, deadlift, bench, squat) building over a 3-week wave, then a deload, plus light assistance work each day.
notes: Percentages are of your training max (about 90% of a true 1-rep max), not your actual max — this is what keeps the program sustainable over many cycles. Repeat this 4-week cycle, raising your training max by about 5 lb (upper body) or 10 lb (lower body) after each deload.

weeks.title: Week 1
weeks.notes: Work up to sets of 5/5/5+ at roughly 65/75/85% of your training max on the main lift; the last set is AMRAP (as many reps as possible).

weeks.title: Week 2
weeks.notes: Work up to sets of 3/3/3+ at roughly 70/80/90% of your training max; the last set is AMRAP.

weeks.title: Week 3
weeks.notes: The heaviest week: sets of 5/3/1+ at roughly 75/85/95% of your training max; the last set is AMRAP.

weeks.title: Week 4
weeks.notes: Light sets of 5/5/5 at roughly 40/50/60% of your training max, no AMRAP — recover, then raise your training max slightly and start the next cycle.

name: GZCLP
description: Cody Lefever's beginner-to-intermediate linear progression built on three tiers per day: a heavy T1 lift (5x3+), a moderate T2 lift (3x10), and a light T3 accessory (3x15+).
notes: T1 (5 sets of 3 + AMRAP): add weight if the AMRAP set hit 3+ reps, otherwise repeat the weight; after 3 failures, drop to a lighter 3x5 stage before returning to 5x3. T2 (3 sets of 10): add weight when all 3 sets hit 10. T3 (3 sets, last set AMRAP): add weight when the AMRAP set passes 25 reps.

name: PHUL (Power Hypertrophy Upper Lower)
description: A 4-day split pairing two heavy, low-rep power days with two higher-rep hypertrophy days — strength and muscle size in the same week.
notes: Power days chase weight on the bar within the given rep range; hypertrophy days chase quality reps and a pump, adding weight only once every set at the top of the rep range feels controlled.

weeks.notes: Add a small amount of weight to each lift every session you complete all reps; if a lift fails three sessions in a row, deload it 10% and build back up.

name: PHAT (Power Hypertrophy Adaptive Training)
description: Layne Norton's 5-day advanced split: two heavy power days followed by three higher-volume hypertrophy days targeting back/shoulders, legs, and chest/arms.
notes: This is a demanding, high-frequency split — it assumes solid technique on the main barbell lifts already. Power days prioritize adding weight; hypertrophy days prioritize volume and a controlled pump.

name: Arnold Split (6-Day)
description: The classic 'golden era' bodybuilding split popularized by Arnold Schwarzenegger: Chest & Back, Shoulders & Arms, and Legs, each trained twice a week.
notes: A high-volume, high-frequency split built for lifters with a training history and the recovery capacity for 6 sessions a week. Take your rest day wherever it fits your schedule.
